stories.headline: Colorado activist convicted of doxxing Denver police commander in first-of-its-kind case
stories.summary:
A Denver County Court jury convicted Regan Benson, 53, of sharing the personal information of a protected person online after she repeated a Denver police commander's home address during a livestreamed protest and urged followers to gather there for a "pig roast party." The verdict appears to be the first conviction under Colorado's anti-doxxing statute, which prohibits sharing officers' personal information online when doing so poses an imminent and serious threat to their safety.

The law protecting police officers' personal information has been on the books for more than two decades, but drew renewed attention in 2021 and 2022 when the legislature expanded doxxing protections to other professions, including health care workers, animal control officers and code enforcement officers. Benson's attorney, Jamie Hubbard, argued the prosecution was unconstitutional retaliation for Benson's criticism of the Denver Police Department, including a lawsuit she had filed in June.

The misdemeanor conviction carries a potential sentence of up to one year in jail. The case has sparked debate among legal observers and civil liberties advocates about whether the state's online privacy statute conflicts with First Amendment free speech protections.

stories.headline: Two fatal crashes on Denver-area highways kill two, injure several Saturday morning
stories.summary:
Two separate fatal crashes struck Denver-area highways early Saturday, prompting extended lane closures and traffic delays. The first crash occurred around 3:45 a.m. on northbound Interstate 25 at East 58th Avenue in Adams County, where a Chevrolet SUV struck a Mitsubishi SUV and then a Mercedes-Benz collided with the Chevrolet while its occupants were outside the vehicle. A 30-year-old man from the Chevrolet was pronounced dead at a local hospital, a 20-year-old man from the same vehicle was hospitalized in serious condition, and the Mercedes driver was also transported to a hospital.

The second crash occurred on westbound Interstate 70 near Kipling Street in Wheat Ridge, where a single-vehicle crash killed one person and sent two others to the hospital, one in serious condition. Wheat Ridge police closed four lanes of the highway near Wadsworth Boulevard for several hours; all lanes reopened by early afternoon.

The Colorado State Patrol and Wheat Ridge police are each investigating their respective crashes. Both agencies asked motorists to seek alternate routes during the closures.

stories.headline: Federal Heights police and fire chiefs ousted amid ongoing tension with city leadership
stories.summary:
Federal Heights Police Chief Robert Grado and Fire Chief Marc Mahoney were fired this week following months of escalating conflict between the city's public safety agencies and city management. City officials announced the departures in an email to employees Friday evening, describing the chiefs as having resigned, but union representatives for both the police and fire departments said neither chief submitted a resignation and that they were not notified of any termination.

The firings follow a unanimous no-confidence vote in City Manager Jacqueline Halburnt submitted by both unions to the City Council in late January. Union officials said tensions began after police department leaders sought funding to hire additional officers to meet national staffing standards, and they characterized the chiefs' removal as retaliation. Deputy Chief Karl Ballard was named interim police chief and Finance Director Tim Weitzman will oversee the fire department.

The unions announced they will request a state investigation into the terminations, calling the manner in which the chiefs were removed "incredibly disrespectful." The Federal Heights Police Department had previously drawn scrutiny in 2023 when the 17th Judicial District Attorney found the agency had failed to investigate serious felony cases for several years, though union officials said conditions had dramatically improved since then.

stories.headline: JBS Greeley meatpacking workers to return Tuesday after company agrees to resume negotiations
stories.summary:
Thousands of striking workers at the JBS meatpacking plant in Greeley will return to their jobs starting at 5 a.m. Tuesday after the company agreed to return to the bargaining table, United Food and Commercial Workers Local 7 announced Saturday evening. The strike, which began March 16 and involved approximately 3,800 workers, marked the first walkout at an American meatpacking plant in four decades and the first-ever strike at the Greeley facility.

UFCW Local 7 President Kim Cordova said JBS agreed to resume negotiations Thursday and Friday. Workers walked out over the company's refusal to increase minimum wages and what the union described as unfair labor practices during contract negotiations. JBS disputed those characterizations, saying union leaders left the table and that the company had made meaningful movement during talks.

The Greeley plant processes as much as 8 percent of the country's beef supply, and an extended strike threatened broader supply disruptions and higher consumer prices. JBS USA said it is "preparing to resume and ramp up operations" and that its last, best and final contract offer remains on the table, with the company expressing hope employees will have the opportunity to vote on it soon.

stories.headline: Metro Denver home and condo sales surge 28% in March, outpacing prior-year gains
stories.summary:
Metro Denver home and condo sales jumped 28.1 percent between February and March, reaching 3,631 closings, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' monthly market report. The increase outpaced the 25.4 percent gain recorded in March 2025 and the 13.4 percent gain in March 2024, a notable rebound after the weakest January for home sales since 2008.

The median number of days a listing spent on the market fell to 16 from 32 in February, and pending sales climbed 30.7 percent to 4,612, with pending transactions running 5.1 percent above year-ago levels. The median price of a single-family home rose to $645,000, up 2.7 percent from February, while condo and townhome prices rebounded to a median of $397,000, a 4.5 percent monthly gain.

Despite the strong showing, headwinds remain. Thirty-year mortgage rates climbed back to 6.46 percent after dipping below 5 percent in February, driven by bond market reactions to the resumption of U.S.-Iran hostilities and the closing of the Strait of Hormuz. Active inventory rose 9.6 percent to 9,846 listings, keeping buyers with more options than in recent tight-market years.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature advances social media search warrant law, renames Cesar Chavez Day, considers payday lending rules
stories.summary:
Governor Jared Polis signed legislation Monday requiring large social media platforms to comply with state law enforcement search warrants within 72 hours and to create streamlined processes for filing those warrants. The bill, Senate Bill 11, comes roughly a year after Polis vetoed a broader set of social media regulations that had included a similar provision. Polis said the law would help law enforcement track stolen property and protect Coloradans from dangerous drugs before digital trails go cold.

Separately, Polis signed a bill renaming the state holiday formerly known as Cesar Chavez Day to Farm Workers Day, following revelations about Chavez's conduct. The legislation passed both chambers unanimously and was sponsored by House Majority Leader Monica Duran and three other Democrats. The signing took place in a private ceremony, and the change takes effect beginning with the March 31 holiday.

Legislators are also debating whether so-called earned-wage access products — which allow workers to draw advances on their paychecks for fees that can translate to annual interest rates of 365 percent — should be regulated as loans under a 2018 Colorado voter initiative capping payday lending rates. Consumer advocates argue the products are essentially payday loans; industry groups disagree. The debate marks the second consecutive year lawmakers have taken up the question.

stories.headline: Jokic scores 40, leads Nuggets past Spurs 136-134 in overtime to snap San Antonio's 11-game winning streak
stories.summary:
Nikola Jokic posted 40 points, 13 assists and eight rebounds Saturday as the Denver Nuggets defeated the San Antonio Spurs 136-134 in overtime at Ball Arena, snapping San Antonio's 11-game winning streak. Christian Braun added 21 points, Jamal Murray finished with 15 points and 10 assists, and Aaron Gordon scored 15 — including a tying basket with 6.2 seconds left in regulation — before forcing Victor Wembanyama into a miss on San Antonio's final shot of regulation.

The Nuggets rallied from a 13-point fourth-quarter deficit, holding the Spurs to 33 percent shooting over the final 14 minutes including overtime. Jokic accounted for 16 of his points in that span. Wembanyama finished with 34 points, 18 rebounds, seven assists and five blocks for the Spurs, who had been 48-2 this season when holding double-digit leads in the fourth quarter.

The win is Denver's second comeback victory over San Antonio from a 13-point fourth-quarter hole this season. The Nuggets, who earlier this week clinched their eighth consecutive NBA playoff berth, host Portland on Monday.

stories.headline: Avalanche shut out Stars 2-0, become first NHL team to 50 wins; extend Central Division lead to eight points
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Dallas Stars 2-0 on Saturday at American Airlines Center, with Scott Wedgewood making 17 saves for his third shutout of the season. Martin Necas scored the go-ahead goal and added an assist, while Nathan MacKinnon also scored for Colorado, which became the first team in the NHL to reach 50 wins this season.

The victory extended Colorado's lead atop the Central Division to eight points over the second-place Stars, with the Avalanche having played two fewer games. The win came despite the Avalanche playing without injured defenseman Cale Makar, who is expected to miss several more games. Head coach Jared Bednar described the game as a "playoff hockey game" and noted that one play has separated the two closely matched teams in every meeting this season.

Dallas fell to 1-2-1 in its past four games and 2-5-2 in its last nine. Both teams have already clinched playoff berths and are expected to meet again in the postseason.

stories.headline: Vail Resort to close Wednesday, 11 days ahead of schedule, citing warm temperatures and poor snowpack
stories.summary:
Vail Resort announced Friday it will close for the 2025-26 ski season on Wednesday, April 8, more than 11 days ahead of its previously scheduled April 19 closing date. Resort officials cited warm temperatures and deteriorating spring conditions despite a fresh foot of snow that fell Thursday night. Vail will join at least 17 other Colorado ski areas that have already closed or plan to close this weekend.

The early closure comes amid what officials have described as Colorado's worst snowpack since statewide recordkeeping began in 1941. Climate scientists have projected that Colorado's ski season will shorten by several weeks in coming decades as warming temperatures cause precipitation to fall increasingly as rain rather than snow.

The announcement adds to a difficult season for Colorado's ski industry, which has seen widespread early closures across the state. Resorts that remain open are expected to wind down operations over the next several weeks as conditions continue to deteriorate.

stories.headline: Four Mile Historic Park Easter egg hunt draws thousands of Denver families for holiday weekend
stories.summary:
Four Mile Historic Park's annual Easter egg hunt drew more than 2,000 ticket holders Saturday, with families lining up before the gates opened at 8:30 a.m. for two separate sessions. Activities included live music, face painting, a hay ride, a magic show, animal encounters featuring horses, pigs and goats, and four separate egg hunts divided by age group — including, for the first time, a hunt for adults offering gift cards and drink tickets as prizes.

Miss Colorado 2025 Gabrielle Gramont made a special appearance, greeting children and posing for photos throughout the event. The park, located in southeast Denver, used the occasion to showcase its historic grounds and farm animals to multigenerational family groups, including several with out-of-town visitors.

The event continues a longstanding Denver tradition at one of the city's oldest sites, the Four Mile House, a stagecoach stop dating to 1859. Organizers said golden eggs hidden throughout the grounds contained vouchers for additional prizes, giving children extra incentive to search carefully.

stories.headline: Trump executive order targeting mail-in voting puts Colorado's election system in federal crosshairs
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump issued an executive order this week seeking to transfer oversight of mail-in ballot procedures from state and local election officials to the Department of Homeland Security and the U.S. Postal Service, a move that directly challenges Colorado's election model. Colorado adopted universal mail-in voting in 2013 under then-Gov. John Hickenlooper, and today more than 90 percent of the state's voters cast ballots by mail — a system widely regarded as a national model.

Legal experts broadly expect federal courts to reject the order on constitutional grounds, as the Constitution assigns responsibility for administering elections to individual states. Colorado's mail-in voting law was crafted in part by the Colorado County Clerks Association, a group predominantly composed of Republican officeholders, and the approach has since drawn bipartisan support within the state.

The executive order adds to ongoing national debate over voting access and election security. Colorado officials have not yet commented publicly on the order, but the state's election infrastructure — including same-day voter registration and automatic mail ballot delivery to all active registered voters — would face significant disruption if federal oversight were imposed.

joke.joke: My doctor told me to watch what I eat, so now I eat in front of the TV.

topStory.headline: U.S. Rescues Second F-15 Crew Member From Iranian Territory as War Spreads Across Region
topStory.summary:
A missing U.S. service member has been rescued from Iranian territory after an F-15E Strike Eagle was shot down by Iran on Friday, President Donald Trump announced early Sunday in a social media post. Trump said the aviator, who is injured but expected to recover, had taken refuge in what he described as 'the treacherous mountains of Iran.' The rescue operation involved dozens of aircraft and continuous aerial surveillance over a 24-hour period, according to Trump. A second crew member from the same aircraft had been recovered earlier.

The downed F-15E was the first U.S. aircraft to crash in Iranian territory since the conflict began in late February. Iran had offered a reward for anyone who turned in the 'enemy pilot,' underscoring the high stakes of the search-and-rescue operation. The Israeli military separately announced Sunday that its air defenses had been activated and that recent strikes focused on Iranian air defense, drone, and missile systems, following the downing of two U.S. warplanes.

Iran extended its attacks Sunday to Gulf state infrastructure, with drone strikes igniting fires at multiple Kuwait Petroleum Corporation facilities, including sites operated by the Kuwait National Petroleum Company and the Petrochemical Industries Company. Bahrain's state-run petrochemical facilities were also reported struck. The United Arab Emirates said 60 projectiles were fired at the country, including nine ballistic missiles, 50 drones, and one cruise missile, bringing the total projectiles targeting the UAE since the war's start to more than 2,700.

Diplomatic pressure mounted Sunday as Oman hosted talks between deputy foreign ministers and experts from the United States and Iran to discuss proposals for ensuring passage through the Strait of Hormuz. The meeting came two days before a deadline set by Trump, who threatened 'devastating consequences' if Iran fails to open the strategically critical waterway by Monday. Oman has historically served as an intermediary between Washington and Tehran.

On the humanitarian and infrastructure front, an internet monitoring group reported Sunday that Iran's internet blackout has now lasted 37 consecutive days, surpassing all other nation-scale shutdowns on record. Journalists from the Associated Press witnessed widespread destruction at a Shiite religious complex in the northwestern Iranian city of Zanjan, and Lebanon's Health Ministry reported casualties after Israeli strikes hit the town of Kfar Hatta near Sidon, a location sheltering large numbers of internally displaced people.
